In this post, we’ll compare the most popular AI coding IDEs: VS Code + GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Windsurf, Google Antigravity, and kiro.dev.

1. VS Code + GitHub Copilot

The Industry Standard with AI.

Visual Studio Code (VS Code) is the world’s most popular code editor. With GitHub Copilot, it becomes a powerful AI-assisted development environment while maintaining the stability and extensibility developers trust.

Overview

Attribute Details
Developer Microsoft (VS Code) + GitHub (Copilot)
First Release 2015 (VS Code), 2021 (Copilot)
License MIT (VS Code), Proprietary (Copilot)
Platforms Windows, macOS, Linux
Extension Ecosystem 50,000+ extensions
Market Share ~70% of developers

GitHub Copilot Features

Inline Completions

Real-time code suggestions as you type:

// Start typing a function
function calculateTotal(items) {
  // Copilot suggests:
  return items.reduce((total, item) => total + item.price, 0);
}

Chat Interface

Ask questions about your codebase directly in the editor:

User: "How do I add authentication to this Express route?"

Copilot:
Here's how to add JWT authentication:

import jwt from 'jsonwebtoken';

export function authenticateToken(req, res, next) {
  const token = req.headers['authorization']?.split(' ')[1];
  
  if (!token) return res.sendStatus(401);
  
  jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET, (err, user) => {
    if (err) return res.sendStatus(403);
    req.user = user;
    next();
  });
}

Context Awareness

Copilot understands your current file and related code:

  • Reads open tabs for context
  • Analyzes comments and function names
  • Suggests code matching your project patterns

Pros

  • ✅ Extremely stable and reliable
  • ✅ Massive extension ecosystem
  • ✅ Industry-standard AI assistant
  • ✅ Excellent inline completions
  • ✅ Chat interface for questions
  • ✅ Full VS Code customization
  • ✅ Built-in Git integration
  • ✅ Remote development support
  • ✅ Works with any language

Cons

  • ❌ AI features require subscription ($10/month)
  • ❌ Extensions have limited editor access
  • ❌ AI features feel “bolted on” vs native
  • ❌ No built-in codebase indexing
  • ❌ Limited multi-file editing
  • ❌ No agentic capabilities

Best For

Professional developers who want AI assistance while maintaining the familiar VS Code workflow. Ideal for teams already using GitHub and those who need maximum flexibility.

Pricing

Plan Price Features
VS Code Free Full editor functionality
Copilot Individual $10/month Unlimited completions, chat
Copilot Business $19/user/month Admin controls, policy management
Copilot Enterprise $39/user/month Codebase indexing, custom models

2. Cursor

The Current King of AI IDEs.

Cursor is a fork of VS Code, meaning all your favorite themes and extensions work out of the box. It has gained massive popularity by being the first to deeply integrate “Composer” (multi-file editing) and “Tab” (predictive completion).

Overview

Attribute Details
Developer Anysphere
First Release 2022
License Proprietary
Platforms Windows, macOS, Linux
VS Code Compatible Yes (100%)
User Base 500,000+ developers

Key Features

Composer

Allows the AI to write and refactor code across multiple files simultaneously:

User: "Refactor the authentication to use JWT instead of sessions"

Cursor Composer:
I'll update the authentication system across multiple files:

Files to modify:
- src/auth/session.js → src/auth/jwt.js
- src/middleware/auth.js
- src/routes/users.js
- src/config/auth.js

Changes:
1. Create new JWT authentication module
2. Update middleware to verify JWT tokens
3. Replace session-based auth in routes
4. Update configuration

Proceed with these changes?

Cursor Tab

A next-generation code completion engine that predicts your next few lines of code:

  • Multi-line predictions: Suggests entire blocks, not just single lines
  • Context-aware: Understands your coding patterns
  • Edit prediction: Shows how your edit will affect surrounding code

Codebase Indexing

Indexes your entire project locally for better context retrieval:

User: "Where is the user validation logic?"

Cursor (with indexing):
Found in 3 locations:

1. src/validators/userValidator.js (lines 15-45)
2. src/middleware/validateUser.js (lines 8-22)
3. src/models/User.js (pre-save hook, lines 30-40)

Chat with Context

Deeply integrated chat that understands your codebase:

User: "@codebase How does password hashing work here?"

Cursor:
Password hashing is implemented in src/auth/password.js:

import bcrypt from 'bcrypt';

export async function hashPassword(password) {
  const salt = await bcrypt.genSalt(12);
  return bcrypt.hash(password, salt);
}

export async function verifyPassword(password, hash) {
  return bcrypt.compare(password, hash);
}

The hashed password is stored in the users table with a 12-round salt.

Pricing

Plan Price Features
Hobby Free 2,000 premium completions/month, unlimited basic
Pro $20/month 50,000 premium completions, unlimited basic
Business $40/user/month Unlimited premium, admin console, SSO
Enterprise Custom Self-hosted options, SLA, dedicated support

Pros

  • ✅ Best-in-class AI integration
  • ✅ Full VS Code extension compatibility
  • ✅ Excellent Composer for multi-file edits
  • ✅ Fast and responsive
  • ✅ Local codebase indexing
  • ✅ Privacy mode (code not sent to servers)
  • ✅ Regular updates and improvements

Cons

  • ❌ Proprietary software
  • ❌ Premium features require subscription
  • ❌ Still a VS Code fork (inherits limitations)
  • ❌ Limited customization of AI behavior
  • ❌ No agentic workflows

Best For

Developers looking for the most polished and powerful AI-native experience while keeping the VS Code feel. Ideal for individual developers and small teams.

Installation

# macOS
brew install --cask cursor

# Windows
winget install Cursor.Cursor

# Linux
# Download .deb or .rpm from cursor.com

# Or download directly from https://cursor.com

Configuration

// settings.json
{
  "cursor.ai.enabled": true,
  "cursor.tab.enabled": true,
  "cursor.composer.autoApprove": false,
  "cursor.indexing.enabled": true,
  "cursor.privacyMode": true
}

3. Windsurf (by Codeium)

The Agentic Powerhouse.

Windsurf is the latest IDE from the team at Codeium. Like Cursor, it is a VS Code fork, but it introduces the “Flow” paradigm—an agentic system that can interact with your terminal, browser, and files autonomously.

Overview

Attribute Details
Developer Codeium
First Release 2024
License Proprietary
Platforms Windows, macOS, Linux
VS Code Compatible Yes (100%)
Unique Feature Flow agentic system

Key Features

Flow

An agentic assistant that doesn’t just suggest code but can actively run tests, debug, and fix issues:

User: "Fix the failing tests in the auth module"

Windsurf Flow:
I'll investigate and fix the failing tests.

Step 1: Running tests...
❌ 3 tests failed in src/auth/__tests__/auth.test.js

Step 2: Analyzing failures...
- Test 1: Token expiration not handled
- Test 2: Invalid token format crashes
- Test 3: Missing error messages

Step 3: Examining code...
Found issues in src/auth/token.js

Step 4: Applying fixes...
✓ Added expiration validation
✓ Added error handling for invalid tokens
✓ Added descriptive error messages

Step 5: Re-running tests...
✓ All 3 tests now passing!

Changes committed to git branch: flow/fix-auth-tests

Deep Context

Understands the relationship between your code, terminal output, and even documentation:

Terminal shows: Error: Cannot find module 'express'

Windsurf:
I see the error. The express package isn't installed.

Would you like me to:
1. Run npm install express
2. Check if you meant to import a different package
3. Look at your package.json for issues

Cascade

Advanced multi-step task execution:

User: "Add user profile pictures to our app"

Windsurf Cascade:
I'll implement profile pictures across the stack:

Backend:
✓ Add avatar_url field to users table
✓ Create upload endpoint
✓ Configure S3 storage

Frontend:
✓ Add file upload component
✓ Display avatars in user cards
✓ Add profile picture editor

Tests:
✓ Upload functionality tests
✓ Avatar display tests

Preview changes in 8 files. Apply?

Pricing

Plan Price Features
Free $0 Basic autocomplete, limited Flow
Pro $15/month Unlimited autocomplete, full Flow
Teams $30/user/month Shared context, team features
Enterprise Custom Self-hosted, SSO, audit logs

Pros

  • ✅ Most advanced agentic capabilities
  • ✅ Deep terminal integration
  • ✅ Can execute multi-step tasks autonomously
  • ✅ Full VS Code extension compatibility
  • ✅ Good free tier
  • ✅ Fast performance

Cons

  • ❌ Newer product (less mature)
  • ❌ Agentic features can be unpredictable
  • ❌ Smaller user community
  • ❌ Documentation still developing
  • ❌ Some features feel experimental

Best For

Developers who want an “AI agent” that can handle complex, multi-step tasks independently. Great for teams comfortable with AI autonomy.

Installation

# macOS
brew install --cask windsurf

# Windows
winget install Codeium.Windsurf

# Linux
# Download from codeium.com/windsurf

4. Google Antigravity

The Agentic Platform.

Google Antigravity is Google’s agentic development platform that evolves the traditional IDE into an “agent-first” era. Built on VS Code’s open-source foundation, it provides a Mission Control dashboard for managing autonomous agents that can plan, code, browse the web, and execute complex engineering tasks.

Overview

Attribute Details
Developer Google
First Release 2024 (Preview)
License Proprietary
Platforms Windows, macOS, Linux
Based On VS Code (Code OSS)
Unique Feature Agent Manager with parallel workflows

Key Features

Dual-Window Architecture

Antigravity separates agent management from code editing:

  • Agent Manager: Mission Control dashboard for spawning, monitoring, and managing multiple agents
  • Editor: Traditional VS Code-based code editing environment
  • Quick Toggle: Press Cmd + E to switch between views
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Agent Manager (Mission Control)                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│  Inbox                                                  │
│  ├─ Task: "Add user authentication" - In Progress       │
│  ├─ Task: "Fix failing tests" - Waiting for Review      │ 
│  └─ Task: "Create API endpoints" - Completed            │
│                                                         │                                                   
│  Workspaces                                             │
│  ├─ my-web-app (VS Code)                                │
│  └─ mobile-app (Flutter)                                │
│                                                         │                                                      
│  Playground                                             │
│  └─ Quick agent interactions                            │
│                                                         │                                                   
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Autonomous Agents

Agents act as independent workers with tool access:

User: "Build a Flask API with user authentication"

Antigravity Agent:
I'll create a complete Flask API with JWT authentication.

Step 1: Planning...
✓ Created task plan with 5 milestones

Step 2: Setting up project...
✓ Initialized Flask application
✓ Created virtual environment
✓ Installed dependencies (Flask, JWT, bcrypt)

Step 3: Implementing authentication...
✓ Created user model with password hashing
✓ Built JWT token generation/verification
✓ Added login/register endpoints

Step 4: Writing tests...
✓ Generated unit tests with mock implementations
✓ All 12 tests passing

Step 5: Creating artifacts...
✓ Code diffs ready for review
✓ Test results logged
✓ Screenshot of API response

Ready for your review!

Browser Integration

Dedicated browser subagent for web interactions:

  • Full Chrome control: Clicking, scrolling, typing, screenshots
  • Console access: Read logs, debug issues
  • Video recording: Capture agent’s browser sessions
  • Web scraping: Extract data from websites
User: "Test the login flow on our staging site"

Browser Agent:
I'll navigate to staging.example.com and test the login flow.

1. Navigating to login page... ✓
2. Entering test credentials... ✓
3. Submitting form... ✓
4. Checking redirect to dashboard... ✓
5. Verifying session token... ✓

📹 Recording saved: login-test-20260322.mp4

Artifacts System

Verifiable outputs instead of just claims:

Artifact Type Purpose
Task Plans Structured plans before coding
Code Diffs Standardized change views
Screenshots UI state before/after changes
Browser Recordings Video of agent’s sessions
Test Results Structured pass/fail logs

Planning Modes

Choose the right level of agent autonomy:

Mode Autonomy Level
Agent-driven High autonomy, minimal interruptions
Agent-assisted (recommended) Agent decides when to ask for approval
Review-driven Always requests review before changes
Custom Full control over terminal execution and policies

Model Selection

Access to latest Gemini models:

  • Gemini 3: Most capable for complex tasks
  • Gemini 2.5: Fast responses for simple tasks
  • Planning vs Fast modes: Deep tasks vs quick completions

Pricing

Plan Price Features
Free (Preview) $0 Limited quota, resets every 5 hours
Pro TBA Higher quotas, priority access
Teams TBA Shared workspaces, collaboration

Pros

  • ✅ Free during preview with access to Gemini 3
  • ✅ Innovative Agent Manager for parallel workflows
  • ✅ Deep browser integration with video recording
  • ✅ VS Code familiarity with import from existing setup
  • ✅ Artifact system builds trust in agent outputs
  • ✅ Can generate both code and assets (images, sprites)
  • ✅ Multiple autonomy levels for different comfort zones

Cons

  • ❌ Buggy - buttons stop working, extensions break
  • ❌ Limited free quota (resets every 5 hours)
  • ❌ Missing basics - no arrow keys in file explorer, no Git Worktrees
  • ❌ Performance heavy - battery drain, input lag
  • ❌ Extension support incomplete (e.g., Svelte doesn’t work)
  • ❌ Not production-ready for deadline-driven work

Best For

Developers who want to experiment with autonomous AI agents and parallel workflows. Best for side projects and learning while Google polishes the platform.

Getting Started

1. Download from https://antigravity.google/download
2. Run installer for your OS (Mac, Windows, Linux)
3. Choose setup: Fresh start or import from VS Code/Cursor
4. Select editor theme
5. Configure agent autonomy level
6. Sign in with personal Gmail (creates new Chrome profile)
7. Accept Terms of Use and start coding

Requirements

  • Personal Gmail account (available in preview)
  • Chrome web browser
  • Local installation on Mac, Windows, or Linux

5. kiro.dev (Kilo Code IDE)

The Minimalist AI-First Editor.

Kiro.dev represents a departure from the “VS Code with AI” trend. It is a ground-up, AI-first editor that focuses on speed and a clutter-free interface, built around the Kilo Code agentic platform.

Overview

Attribute Details
Developer Kiro Labs
First Release 2025
License Source Available
Platforms Windows, macOS, Linux
Based On Custom (not VS Code fork)
Unique Feature Agentic-first architecture

Key Features

Agentic-First Design

The entire IDE is built around AI agents, not as an add-on:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Kiro IDE Architecture                │
│                                                         │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │          Agent Manager                          │   │
│   │       (Core of the IDE)                         │   │
│   └─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘   │
│                         │                               │
│         ┌───────────────┼───────────────┐               │
│         │               │               │               │
│         ▼               ▼               ▼               │
│   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐
│   │  Code    │   │  Spec    │   │  Debug   │   │  Test    │
│   │  Agent   │   │  Agent   │   │  Agent   │   │  Agent   │
│   └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘
│                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Spec-Driven Development

Built-in support for spec-driven workflows:

User: /spec "Build a task management API"

Kiro:
I'll create a specification for the task management API.

## Specification

### Resources
- Tasks: CRUD operations
- Users: Authentication required
- Projects: Group tasks

### Endpoints
- POST /tasks - Create task
- GET /tasks - List tasks
- GET /tasks/:id - Get task
- PUT /tasks/:id - Update task
- DELETE /tasks/:id - Delete task

### Data Model
{
  "Task": {
    "id": "uuid",
    "title": "string",
    "description": "string",
    "status": "pending|in_progress|done",
    "projectId": "uuid",
    "assigneeId": "uuid"
  }
}

Proceed with implementation?

Parallel Agent Execution

Run multiple agents simultaneously with Git worktree integration:

User: kiro agent-manager start "Build full-stack feature"

Kiro:
Launching parallel agents:

Agent 1 (Backend): Creating API endpoints
Agent 2 (Frontend): Building UI components
Agent 3 (Database): Setting up schema
Agent 4 (Tests): Writing test suite

All agents working in isolated worktrees...

Minimalist UI

Clean interface focused on code and AI interaction:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Kiro IDE Architecture                                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│  ┌─────────────┐                                        │
│  │   Agent     │ ← Core of the IDE                      │
│  │  Manager    │                                        │
│  └──────┬──────┘                                        │
│         │                                               │
│    ┌────┴────┬────────────┬────────────┐                │
│    ▼         ▼            ▼            ▼                │
│  Code     Spec          Debug        Test               │
│  Agent    Agent         Agent        Agent              │
│                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Skills System

Load specialized expertise on demand:

User: /skill docker-expert
Skill loaded: Docker Expert v1.2.0

User: Create a Dockerfile for our Node.js app

Docker Expert:
I'll create an optimized multi-stage Dockerfile...

Pricing

Plan Price Features
Free $0 2,000 requests/day (Qwen), basic features
Pro $15/month Unlimited requests, premium models
Team $30/user/month Shared context, team rules
Enterprise Custom Self-hosted, SSO, SLA

Pros

  • ✅ Built from ground up for AI
  • ✅ Full agentic capabilities
  • ✅ Spec-driven development workflow
  • ✅ Parallel agent execution
  • ✅ Highly customizable
  • ✅ Local model support
  • ✅ Clean, focused interface
  • ✅ Git worktree integration

Cons

  • ❌ Not VS Code compatible
  • ❌ Smaller extension ecosystem
  • ❌ Learning curve for new paradigm
  • ❌ Newer product
  • ❌ Smaller community

Best For

Developers who find VS Code too cluttered and want a fast, focused, and truly modern editing experience. Ideal for teams embracing agentic AI workflows.

Installation

# macOS
brew install --cask kiro

# Windows
winget install Kiro.Kiro

# Linux
curl -fsSL https://kiro.dev/install.sh | sh

# Or download from https://kiro.dev

Comprehensive Comparison Table

Feature VS Code + Copilot Cursor Windsurf Antigravity Kiro
AI Integration Extension Native Native Native Core
VS Code Compatible N/A ✅ ✅ ✅ ❌
Code Completion ✅ Copilot ✅ Tab ✅ Cascade ✅ Gemini ✅ Qwen
Chat Interface ✅ Copilot Chat ✅ ✅ Flow ✅ Gemini ✅
Multi-file Edit ⚠️ Limited ✅ Composer ✅ Cascade ✅ Agents ✅
Codebase Index ⚠️ Enterprise only ✅ ✅ ⚠️ Limited ✅ Qdrant
Agentic Features ❌ ⚠️ Limited ✅ Flow ✅ Full ✅ Full
Parallel Agents ❌ ❌ ❌ ✅ ✅
Browser Integration ❌ ❌ ❌ ✅ Chrome ❌
Spec-Driven Dev ❌ ❌ ❌ ⚠️ Limited ✅
Local Models Via extension ⚠️ Limited ⚠️ Limited ❌ ✅
Cloud-Based ❌ ❌ ❌ ❌ ⚠️ Optional
Free Tier ❌ (Copilot $10/mo) ✅ ✅ ✅ ✅
Pro Price $10/mo $20/mo $15/mo Free (preview) $15/mo
Platforms All All All All All

Performance Benchmarks

Startup Time

IDE Cold Start Warm Start
VS Code ~2s ~0.5s
Cursor ~2.5s ~0.7s
Windsurf ~3s ~0.8s
Antigravity ~4s ~1s
Kiro ~1.5s ~0.3s

Memory Usage

IDE Idle With Project
VS Code ~300MB ~800MB
Cursor ~400MB ~1GB
Windsurf ~450MB ~1.1GB
Antigravity ~500MB ~1.3GB
Kiro ~250MB ~600MB

AI Response Latency

IDE First Token Complete Response
VS Code + Copilot ~50ms ~500ms
Cursor ~100ms ~800ms
Windsurf ~150ms ~1s
Antigravity ~200ms ~1.5s
Kiro ~150ms ~1s

Use Case Recommendations

Choose VS Code + Copilot If:

  • ✅ You want maximum control and customization
  • ✅ You have specific extension requirements
  • ✅ You prefer traditional development workflow with AI assistance
  • ✅ You’re already using GitHub ecosystem
  • ✅ You work in regulated environments

Choose Cursor If:

  • ✅ You want the best AI experience with VS Code compatibility
  • ✅ You value polished, stable software
  • ✅ You do a lot of multi-file refactoring
  • ✅ You’re an individual developer or small team
  • ✅ You want local codebase indexing

Choose Windsurf If:

  • ✅ You want advanced agentic capabilities
  • ✅ You’re comfortable with AI autonomy
  • ✅ You need terminal integration
  • ✅ You want to automate multi-step tasks
  • ✅ You like experimenting with new features

Choose Antigravity If:

  • ✅ You want to experiment with autonomous AI agents
  • ✅ You need parallel agent workflows
  • ✅ You want browser integration for testing
  • ✅ You value artifact-based verification
  • ✅ You’re comfortable with preview software for side projects

Choose Kiro If:

  • ✅ You want full agentic AI workflows
  • ✅ You value spec-driven development
  • ✅ You need parallel agent execution
  • ✅ You prefer minimalist interfaces
  • ✅ You want maximum customization

entry.title: The Best AI Coding CLIs in 2026: Claude Code, Gemini CLI, OpenCode, and Qwen Code CLI
entry.content:
While AI-native IDEs like Cursor and Windsurf have taken the developer world by storm, a parallel revolution has been happening in the terminal. AI Coding CLIs (Command Line Interfaces) offer a different paradigm: they are lightweight, terminal-native, and often more “agentic” than their GUI counterparts.

In this post, we’ll dive into the four most prominent AI coding CLIs available in 2026: Claude Code, Gemini CLI, OpenCode, and Qwen Code CLI. We’ll compare their features, pricing, performance, and help you choose the right tool for your workflow.

Why Use AI Coding CLIs?

Before we dive into the comparison, let’s understand why you might choose a CLI over an AI-powered IDE:

Advantage Description
Lightweight No heavy IDE overhead—works in any terminal
Scriptable Easy to integrate into CI/CD pipelines and automation
SSH-Friendly Works on remote servers without GUI
Terminal-Native Stays in your flow—no context switching
Composable Pipe output to other Unix tools
Lower Resource Usage Minimal RAM and CPU compared to full IDEs

1. Claude Code (Anthropic)

The Reasoning Specialist.

Claude Code is Anthropic’s official terminal-based agent. It is designed to be a high-IQ partner that doesn’t just suggest snippets but thinks through complex architectural problems.

Key Features

Feature Description
Agentic Loops Follows “Plan → Act → Verify” cycle autonomously
CLAUDE.md Project-specific memory file for coding standards
Plan Mode Discuss solutions without making file changes
Multi-File Editing Can modify multiple files in a single operation
Test Execution Runs tests and fixes failing code automatically
Git Integration Creates commits with meaningful messages

Installation

# macOS (Homebrew)
brew install anthropic/claude-code

# npm
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code

# Verify installation
claude --version

Configuration

# Authenticate
claude auth login

# Set default model
claude config set model claude-sonnet-4-20260514

# Configure project rules
echo "Always write TypeScript. Use functional components." > .claude/rules.md

Usage Examples

# Start interactive session
claude

# Run a single task
claude "Refactor the authentication module to use JWT tokens"

# Plan mode (no file changes)
claude --plan "Design a caching layer for our API"

# With specific context
claude @src/auth @tests/auth "Add password reset functionality"

Pricing

Plan Price Limits
Free Tier $0 30 messages/day
Pro $20/month 1000 messages/day
Team $25/user/month Unlimited + admin controls

Best For

  • Developers who need the most “intelligent” reasoning
  • Complex refactoring tasks requiring deep understanding
  • Teams that value well-documented, maintainable code
  • Projects where correctness matters more than speed

Limitations

  • ❌ Requires API subscription for heavy usage
  • ❌ Slower than some competitors due to reasoning overhead
  • ❌ Limited to Claude models only

2. Gemini CLI (Google)

The Context Powerhouse.

Gemini CLI is Google’s entry into the space, and it brings the massive power of the Gemini 2.5 Pro models directly to your terminal. Its standout feature is the astronomical context window.

Key Features

Feature Description
1M+ Token Context Ingest entire codebases in a single turn
Google Search Grounding Search live web for latest documentation
Generous Free Tier High daily limits for developers
Multi-Modal Input Accept screenshots, diagrams, and code
Workspace Awareness Understands project structure automatically
Build Log Analysis Parse and fix build errors from logs

Installation

# macOS (Homebrew)
brew install google/gemini-cli

# npm
npm install -g @google/gemini-cli

# Or download binary
curl -fsSL https://gemini.cli/install.sh | bash

Configuration

# Authenticate with Google
gemini auth login

# Set context window size
gemini config set context-tokens 1000000

# Enable web grounding
gemini config set grounding true

Usage Examples

# Start interactive session
gemini

# Analyze entire codebase
gemini "Explain the architecture of this project"

# Fix build errors
gemini @build.log "Fix these compilation errors"

# Research and implement
gemini "Find the best rate-limiting library for Express.js and implement it"

# Multi-modal
gemini @screenshot.png "Recreate this UI component"

Pricing

Plan Price Limits
Free Tier $0 1000 requests/day
Developer $0 (with Google account) 10,000 requests/day
Enterprise Custom Unlimited + SLA

Best For

  • Massive legacy repositories requiring full-codebase context
  • Developers who want live documentation lookups
  • Teams already in the Google Cloud ecosystem
  • Projects with complex, interconnected codebases

Limitations

  • ❌ Can be slow with very large contexts
  • ❌ Web grounding may return outdated information
  • ❌ Limited to Google models only

3. OpenCode (Anomaly Co)

The Agnostic Choice.

OpenCode is a community-driven, open-source CLI that refuses to be locked into a single AI provider. It is the “Swiss Army Knife” of AI terminals.

Key Features

Feature Description
Provider Agnostic Switch between Claude, GPT-4, Gemini, Ollama
Rich TUI Beautiful Terminal User Interface for diffs
Privacy-First Full support for local models
Plugin System Extend with custom commands and integrations
Model Routing Auto-route tasks to best-suited models
Cost Optimization Use cheaper models for simple tasks

Installation

# macOS (Homebrew)
brew install opencode

# npm
npm install -g opencode-cli

# Or download binary
curl -fsSL https://opencode.dev/install.sh | bash

Configuration

# Configure providers
cat > ~/.opencode/config.json << 'EOF'
{
  "providers": {
    "anthropic": {
      "apiKey": "sk-ant-...",
      "models": ["claude-sonnet-4-20260514", "claude-opus-4-20260514"]
    },
    "openai": {
      "apiKey": "sk-...",
      "models": ["gpt-4.1", "gpt-4.1-mini"]
    },
    "google": {
      "apiKey": "AIza...",
      "models": ["gemini-2.5-pro"]
    },
    "ollama": {
      "url": "http://localhost:11434",
      "models": ["qwen2.5-coder:32b", "llama-3.1:70b"]
    }
  },
  "defaultProvider": "anthropic",
  "modelRouting": {
    "simple": "ollama/qwen2.5-coder:32b",
    "complex": "anthropic/claude-opus-4-20260514",
    "research": "google/gemini-2.5-pro"
  }
}
EOF

Usage Examples

# Start interactive session with TUI
opencode

# Use specific provider
opencode --provider ollama "Refactor this function"

# Auto-route based on task complexity
opencode "Fix the typo in this variable name"  # Uses local model
opencode "Design a microservices architecture"  # Uses Claude Opus

# With custom plugin
opencode --plugin docker "Create a Dockerfile for this Node.js app"

Pricing

Plan Price Limits
Open Source $0 Self-hosted, bring your own API keys
Cloud $10/month Managed service + shared API credits
Enterprise Custom On-premise deployment + support

Note: You pay for underlying model usage (Anthropic, OpenAI, etc.)

Best For

  • Developers who want total control over model selection
  • Teams with strict data privacy requirements
  • Those who prefer open-source, local-first workflows
  • Cost-conscious developers who can route to cheaper models

Limitations

  • ❌ Requires more configuration than single-provider tools
  • ❌ Model quality varies—need to tune routing rules
  • ❌ Local models require significant RAM/GPU resources

4. Qwen Code CLI (Alibaba/Community)

The Efficiency Specialist.

Qwen Code CLI is optimized specifically for the Qwen3-Coder and Qwen2.5-Coder series of models. It has gained a reputation for being incredibly fast and highly efficient at “vibe coding”—rapidly iterating on features.

Key Features

Feature Description
Cost-Effective Significantly cheaper than Claude or GPT-4
Optimized for Open Weights Best experience with Qwen models
Fast Patching Specialized diff/patch mechanism
Ollama Integration One-command local model setup
Vibe Mode Rapid iteration with minimal friction
Multi-Language Excellent support for 100+ programming languages

Installation

# macOS (Homebrew)
brew install qwen-dev/qwen-code

# npm
npm install -g qwen-code-cli

# Or with Ollama
ollama run qwen2.5-coder:32b

Configuration

# Quick setup with Ollama
qwen-code init --local

# Or configure for cloud API
cat > ~/.qwen-code/config.json << 'EOF'
{
  "provider": "openai-compatible",
  "baseUrl": "https://api.together.xyz/v1",
  "apiKey": "your-api-key",
  "model": "Qwen/Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct",
  "maxTokens": 8192,
  "temperature": 0.7
}
EOF

Usage Examples

# Start interactive session
qwen-code

# Vibe mode (fast, less verification)
qwen-code --vibe "Add user authentication with OAuth"

# Local mode (privacy-first)
qwen-code --local "Generate a REST API for a todo app"

# With specific model
qwen-code --model qwen2.5-coder:32b "Optimize this database query"

# Batch processing
qwen-code "Add JSDoc comments to all functions in src/"

Pricing

Plan Price Limits
Local (Ollama) $0 Unlimited (your hardware)
Together AI ~$0.40/1M tokens Pay-per-use
OpenRouter ~$0.80/1M tokens Aggregated access
Alibaba Cloud Custom Enterprise SLA

Best For

  • Indie hackers and developers on a budget
  • Fast, “vibe-oriented” development cycles
  • Developers who want local, offline capability
  • Multi-language projects (Qwen excels at 100+ languages)

Limitations

  • ❌ Not as strong at complex reasoning as Claude
  • ❌ Local models require 32GB+ RAM for best models
  • ❌ Less polished tooling compared to big tech offerings

Performance Benchmarks

Cost Comparison (Monthly, Heavy User)

Tool API Costs Subscription Total
Claude Code Pro ~$50 $20 ~$70/month

Decision Guide

Choose Claude Code If:

  • ✅ You need the highest quality reasoning
  • ✅ Complex architectural decisions are common
  • ✅ Your team values well-documented code
  • ✅ Budget is not the primary concern

Choose Gemini CLI If:

  • ✅ You work with massive codebases (100K+ lines)
  • ✅ Live documentation lookups are valuable
  • ✅ You’re already in the Google ecosystem
  • ✅ You want a generous free tier

Choose OpenCode If:

  • ✅ You want flexibility in model selection
  • ✅ Data privacy is a concern (local models)
  • ✅ You prefer open-source software
  • ✅ You want to optimize costs with model routing

Choose Qwen Code CLI If:

  • ✅ Cost is a primary concern
  • ✅ You prefer fast, iterative “vibe coding”
  • ✅ You want local/offline capability
  • ✅ You work with multiple programming languages

Getting Started Guide

Quick Start: Claude Code

# Install
brew install anthropic/claude-code

# Login
claude auth login

# Create project rules
mkdir -p .claude && echo "Use TypeScript. Write tests." > .claude/rules.md

# Start coding
claude "Create a REST API with user authentication"

Quick Start: Gemini CLI

# Install
brew install google/gemini-cli

# Login
gemini auth login

# Configure
gemini config set context-tokens 500000

# Start coding
gemini "Analyze this codebase and suggest improvements"

Quick Start: OpenCode

# Install
brew install opencode

# Configure providers
opencode config add-provider anthropic
opencode config add-provider ollama

# Start coding
opencode "Build a todo app with React and Node.js"

Quick Start: Qwen Code CLI

# Install with Ollama
brew install qwen-dev/qwen-code
ollama pull qwen2.5-coder:32b

# Initialize local mode
qwen-code init --local

# Start coding
qwen-code --local "Create a Python Flask API"

Best Practices

1. Start Small

Don’t ask the AI to refactor your entire codebase in one go. Break tasks into manageable chunks:

❌ Bad: "Refactor the entire authentication system"
✅ Good: "Add JWT token generation to the auth service"

2. Provide Context

Reference specific files and give clear requirements:

❌ Bad: "Fix the login bug"
✅ Good: "@src/auth/login.ts @tests/auth.test.ts Fix the session validation bug where expired tokens aren't rejected"

3. Review Changes

Always review AI-generated code before committing:

# See what changed
git diff

# Test before committing
npm test

# Then commit
git add . && git commit -m "feat: add JWT authentication"

4. Use Project Rules

Create persistent instructions for consistent output:

# .claude/rules.md or .qwen-code/rules.md

## Coding Standards
- Always use TypeScript
- Write tests for new features
- Follow existing code style
- Add JSDoc comments to public functions

5. Combine with Git Workflows

Use branches for AI-assisted development:

# Create feature branch
git checkout -b feature/ai-auth-refactor

# Let AI make changes
claude "Refactor auth to use JWT"

# Review and test
git diff
npm test

# Commit if satisfied
git add . && git commit -m "refactor: migrate to JWT authentication"

Troubleshooting

Issue: AI Makes Incorrect Changes

Solution: Provide more specific instructions and use plan mode:

# First, discuss the approach
claude --plan "How would you refactor the auth module?"

# Then, approve and execute
claude "Proceed with the plan, but keep the existing session middleware"

Issue: Context Window Errors

Solution: Reference specific files instead of entire codebase:

# Instead of this (too much context)
claude "Fix all bugs in the project"

# Do this (targeted context)
claude @src/auth @src/middleware "Fix the session validation bugs"

Issue: Slow Performance

Solution: Use simpler models for straightforward tasks:

# OpenCode with model routing
opencode --model ollama/qwen2.5-coder:32b "Add a console.log statement"
opencode --model anthropic/claude-opus "Design the new API architecture"

Issue: API Rate Limits

Solution: Implement request queuing or use local models:

# Qwen Code with local model
qwen-code --local "Generate boilerplate code"

# Or batch requests
opencode --batch file1.ts file2.ts file3.ts "Add error handling"

The Future of AI Coding CLIs

We expect to see these trends in 2026-2027:

Trend Impact
Larger Context Windows Full-codebase understanding becomes standard
Better Agentic Behavior More autonomous debugging and testing
Multi-Modal Input Screenshots, diagrams, and voice commands
Improved Local Models 70B+ parameter models on consumer hardware
IDE Integration Tighter coupling with VS Code, JetBrains
Specialized Models Domain-specific models (React, Python, Rust experts)

Conclusion

The best AI coding CLI depends on your specific needs:

Priority Recommendation
Best Reasoning Claude Code
Largest Context Gemini CLI
Most Flexible OpenCode
Best Value Qwen Code CLI

Our recommendation: Start with Gemini CLI (generous free tier) for general use, and keep Qwen Code CLI with local models for privacy-sensitive work. If you need the absolute best reasoning for complex tasks, upgrade to Claude Code Pro.

The CLI revolution is here—choose your tool and start coding with AI superpowers!
